title: Ni-O hybridization-driven electronic reconstruction across the superconducting dome in an infinite-layer nickelate
authors: Chi Sin Tang, Shengwei Zeng, Xing Gao, Zhaoyang Luo, Xiongfang Liu, Zhi Shiuh Lim, Saurav Prakash, Ping Yang, Caozheng Diao, Xinmao Yin, Changjian Li, Huajun Liu, Mark B. H. Breese, A. Ariando
abstract: Superconductivity in infinite-layer nickelates has drawn wide interest as a cuprate analogue, yet how the electronic structure evolves with hole doping remains unsettled. Here we map the doping- and temperature-dependent unoccupied states of the La-based infinite-layer nickelate La1-xCaxNiO2 using O K-edge and Ni L-edge x-ray absorption spectroscopy. Superconductivity occurs for 0.18<=x<=0.27. Near x~0.20-0.23, low-energy spectral weight redistributes: Ni3d-dominated states decrease while O2p-hybridized states increase, indicating an orbital-selective crossover in Ni-O covalency. This crossover coincides with a sign reversal of the Hall coefficient and precedes the reduction of the superconducting critical temperature at higher doping. By directly linking transport anomalies and the superconducting dome to a measurable Ni-O orbital reorganization, our results provide a key step toward a unified, orbital-resolved phase diagram for infinite-layer nickelates and a practical route to engineer superconductivity via hybridization control.
summary: This experimental study investigates the electronic structure of infinite-layer nickelates across their superconducting dome using X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy. The researchers found that the superconducting state correlates with a doping-induced, orbital-selective crossover in Ni-O hybridization. This work provides a key orbital-resolved mechanism linking electronic structure changes to the superconducting properties.
main_problem: The study aims to understand how the electronic structure of infinite-layer nickelates evolves with hole doping, specifically linking these changes to the superconducting dome.
main_result: A doping-dependent orbital-selective crossover in Ni-O covalency, evidenced by spectral weight redistribution, was found to coincide with transport anomalies like the Hall coefficient sign reversal and the reduction of the superconducting critical temperature.
method: The research utilized X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy (XAS) at the O K-edge and Ni L-edge, combined with temperature-dependent measurements and X-ray Linear Dichroism (XLD).
model_or_system: The physical system is the infinite-layer nickelate $	ext{La}_{1-x}	ext{Ca}_x	ext{NiO}_2$, which serves as a cuprate analogue for studying high-temperature superconductivity.
key_observables: O K-edge spectral weight redistribution (especially features 'a' and 'b'), Ni $L_3$-edge intensity, Hall coefficient sign, and superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$).
important_parameters: Hole doping concentration ($x$), temperature ($T$), and the doping range $0.18 \le x \le 0.27$ where superconductivity occurs.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed spectral weight changes directly reflect orbital reorganization and covalency changes in the Ni-O bonds, and that the pre-peaks are intrinsic electronic features.
figures_summary: Figures summarize the doping dependence of O K-edge spectra across the superconducting dome, showing spectral weight transfer; they also present temperature-dependent measurements and XLD results correlating electronic structure changes with $T_c$.
paper_structure: The paper systematically maps the doping and temperature dependence of electronic states using XAS, correlating the observed orbital reorganization (spectral weight transfer) directly with measurable transport properties (Hall coefficient, $T_c$) across the superconducting dome.
why_interesting: The direct link established between measurable spectroscopic signatures (orbital hybridization) and macroscopic superconducting behavior provides a powerful experimental handle for understanding correlated electron physics in unconventional superconductors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
